---
title: "Can You Beat the Stroop Effect? An Interactive Look at Cognitive Interference"
author: "Example Student"
date: "2026-03-23"
description: "Explore simulated Stroop task data with interactive visualisations — tweak the experiment parameters and watch the data respond."
categories: [neuropsychology]
image: "images/cover.svg"
toc: true
---

::: {.tldr}
- The Stroop effect is one of the most robust findings in cognitive psychology — naming the ink colour of a colour word is slower when word and ink clash
- The interference effect is visible in both reaction times and error rates
- Below you can tweak the experiment and watch the data change in real time
:::

The Stroop task is beautifully simple. You see a word printed in coloured ink and your job is to name the ink colour, not read the word. When the word RED appears in green ink, saying "green" takes measurably longer than when the word GREEN appears in green ink. That delay — typically 80 to 120 ms — is the Stroop effect, and it reveals something fundamental about how automatic reading interferes with controlled colour naming.

Rather than summarise a single paper, this post lets you play with the parameters of a simulated Stroop experiment and see how the data respond.

## Build your own Stroop experiment

Use the sliders to change the experiment. **Interference strength** controls how much slower the incongruent condition is. **Number of participants** changes the sample size. **Individual variability** controls how different people are from each other. Watch the histogram and summary statistics update as you move each slider.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| panel: input

viewof interferenceStrength = Inputs.range(
  [0, 250],
  { value: 120, step: 5, label: "Interference strength (ms)" }
)

viewof nParticipants = Inputs.range(
  [10, 100],
  { value: 40, step: 5, label: "Number of participants" }
)

viewof individualVariability = Inputs.range(
  [10, 120],
  { value: 40, step: 5, label: "Individual variability (SD, ms)" }
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Regenerate data reactively when sliders change
simData = {
  function mulberry32(a) {
    return function() {
      a |= 0; a = a + 0x6D2B79F5 | 0;
      var t = Math.imul(a ^ a >>> 15, 1 | a);
      t = t + Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 7, 61 | t) ^ t;
      return ((t ^ t >>> 14) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
    }
  }

  const rand = mulberry32(42);

  function randn() {
    let u = 0, v = 0;
    while (u === 0) u = rand();
    while (v === 0) v = rand();
    return Math.sqrt(-2.0 * Math.log(u)) * Math.cos(2.0 * Math.PI * v);
  }

  const baseMean = 620;
  const conditions = ["Congruent", "Incongruent"];
  const means = {
    "Congruent": baseMean,
    "Incongruent": baseMean + interferenceStrength
  };
  const withinSD = 80;

  const rows = [];
  let idx = 0;
  for (let pid = 1; pid <= nParticipants; pid++) {
    const offset = randn() * individualVariability;
    for (const cond of conditions) {
      const trialRTs = [];
      for (let t = 0; t < 20; t++) {
        trialRTs.push(Math.max(200, Math.round(means[cond] + offset + randn() * withinSD)));
      }
      const meanRT = Math.round(trialRTs.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / trialRTs.length);
      // Deterministic jitter from index so dots don't overlap
      const jitter = Math.sin(idx * 2.39996) * 15;
      rows.push({ participant: pid, condition: cond, meanRT: meanRT, jitter: jitter });
      idx++;
    }
  }

  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Compute per-participant interference scores
scores = {
  const byP = new Map();
  for (const d of simData) {
    if (!byP.has(d.participant)) byP.set(d.participant, {});
    byP.get(d.participant)[d.condition] = d.meanRT;
  }
  return Array.from(byP.values())
    .map(v => ({ interference: v.Incongruent - v.Congruent }))
    .sort((a, b) => a.interference - b.interference);
}
```

### Distribution of reaction times by condition

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Compute condition medians for the crossbar
conditionMedians = {
  const out = [];
  for (const cond of ["Congruent", "Incongruent"]) {
    const rts = simData.filter(d => d.condition === cond).map(d => d.meanRT).sort((a, b) => a - b);
    const mid = Math.floor(rts.length / 2);
    const median = rts.length % 2 ? rts[mid] : Math.round((rts[mid - 1] + rts[mid]) / 2);
    out.push({ condition: cond, median: median });
  }
  return out;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

Plot.plot({
  height: 380,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 45,
  marginRight: 20,
  style: { fontSize: "13px", fontFamily: "Source Sans 3, sans-serif" },
  color: {
    domain: ["Congruent", "Incongruent"],
    range: ["#28a745", "#9B1B30"],
    legend: true
  },
  x: {
    label: "Condition",
    domain: ["Congruent", "Incongruent"]
  },
  y: { label: "Mean reaction time (ms)", grid: true },
  marks: [
    Plot.dot(simData, {
      x: "condition",
      y: "meanRT",
      fill: "condition",
      fillOpacity: 0.5,
      r: 5,
      dx: "jitter",
      tip: true,
      title: d => `P${d.participant}: ${d.meanRT} ms`
    }),
    Plot.tickX(conditionMedians, {
      x: "condition",
      y: "median",
      stroke: "black",
      strokeWidth: 3,
      strokeOpacity: 0.8
    })
  ]
})
```

### Interference effect per participant

Each bar shows one participant's interference score (Incongruent minus Congruent RT). Bars above the dashed line mean the Stroop effect worked as expected. Try dragging the interference slider to zero — can you eliminate the effect entirely?

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

Plot.plot({
  height: 300,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 45,
  style: { fontSize: "13px", fontFamily: "Source Sans 3, sans-serif" },
  x: {
    label: "Participant (sorted)",
    ticks: []
  },
  y: {
    label: "Interference (ms)",
    grid: true
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([0], { stroke: "#999", strokeDasharray: "4,4" }),
    Plot.barY(scores.map((d, i) => ({...d, i})), {
      x: "i",
      y: "interference",
      fill: d => d.interference >= 0 ? "#9B1B30" : "#28a745",
      fillOpacity: 0.7,
      insetLeft: 0.5,
      insetRight: 0.5,
      tip: true,
      title: d => `${d.interference > 0 ? "+" : ""}${d.interference} ms`
    })
  ]
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Live summary statistics
summaryStats = {
  const conds = ["Congruent", "Incongruent"];
  const out = conds.map(cond => {
    const rts = simData.filter(d => d.condition === cond).map(d => d.meanRT);
    const mean = rts.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / rts.length;
    const sd = Math.sqrt(rts.map(x => (x - mean) ** 2).reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / (rts.length - 1));
    return { condition: cond, mean: Math.round(mean), sd: Math.round(sd), min: Math.min(...rts), max: Math.max(...rts), n: rts.length };
  });

  const ints = scores.map(d => d.interference);
  const intMean = ints.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / ints.length;
  const intSD = Math.sqrt(ints.map(x => (x - intMean) ** 2).reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / (ints.length - 1));
  const tStat = (intMean / (intSD / Math.sqrt(ints.length))).toFixed(2);
  const positive = ints.filter(d => d > 0).length;
  const pctPositive = ((positive / ints.length) * 100).toFixed(0);

  return { conditions: out, intMean: Math.round(intMean), intSD: Math.round(intSD), tStat, pctPositive, n: ints.length };
}
```

## Condition means at a glance

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

html`<table class="fancy-table">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Condition</th>
      <th>Mean RT (ms)</th>
      <th>SD (ms)</th>
      <th>Range (ms)</th>
      <th>N</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    ${summaryStats.conditions.map(d => html`<tr class="${d.condition.toLowerCase()}">
      <td><strong>${d.condition}</strong></td>
      <td>${d.mean}</td>
      <td>${d.sd}</td>
      <td>${d.min}–${d.max}</td>
      <td>${d.n}</td>
    </tr>`)}
    <tr style="border-top: 2px solid #9B1B30;">
      <td><strong>Interference</strong></td>
      <td>${summaryStats.intMean}</td>
      <td>${summaryStats.intSD}</td>
      <td colspan="1"><em>t</em> = ${summaryStats.tStat}</td>
      <td>${summaryStats.pctPositive}% positive</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
  <tfoot>
    <tr>
      <td colspan="5"><em>Simulated data: ${summaryStats.n} participants, 20 trials per condition. All values update live.</em></td>
    </tr>
  </tfoot>
</table>`
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Things to try
- **Set interference to 0** — the bar chart should scatter roughly evenly above and below zero, and the *t*-statistic should drop toward zero. This is what "no effect" looks like.
- **Crank interference to 250** — nearly every bar should be positive and the two dot clouds should separate clearly.
- **Increase individual variability** — the dots spread out and the effect gets harder to detect, even if the interference strength stays the same. This is why noisy data need larger samples.
- **Drop participants to 10, then raise to 100** — watch the *t*-statistic stabilise as the sample grows. This is statistical power in action.
:::

::: {.pullquote}
"Nearly every participant shows a positive interference effect — the Stroop phenomenon is remarkably consistent across individuals."
:::

## Why this matters

The Stroop effect endures because it taps into a fundamental tension in the cognitive system: reading is so automatic that it intrudes even when you are explicitly trying to ignore it. That involuntary interference has made the Stroop task a standard tool for studying attention, executive function, and inhibitory control across clinical and experimental settings.

The interactive above also illustrates something broader: the relationship between effect size, variability, and sample size is not just a statistical abstraction. You can *see* the signal emerge from (or disappear into) the noise as you adjust the sliders. That intuition is harder to build from formulas alone.

---

::: {.author-card}

<div>
<div class="author-card-name">Example Student</div>
<div class="author-card-bio">
  Psychology student at Goldsmiths, University of London.
  Interested in cognitive psychology and interactive data.
</div>
</div>
:::

*This post is published under [CC BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).*
